title: Epic 1.1 — Snapshot Strategy
description:
Verify avt API surface, implement the terminal state snapshot mechanism using `Vt::dump()`/restore, and define the core types used by the keyframe index.

## Context

`avt::Vt` does **not** implement `Clone`. However, `Vt::dump()` serializes the full terminal state (both screen buffers, cursor, pen, modes, charsets, tab stops, scroll margins, saved cursor contexts) into an escape sequence string. Feeding this dump into a fresh `Vt` of the **same dimensions** reproduces the exact state. This is the chosen snapshot strategy.

### Key constraints

- `dump()` does NOT capture terminal dimensions — the caller must track and restore these.
- When primary buffer is active, alternate buffer **content** is not captured (only saved cursor context). This is correct: entering alternate mode always starts a fresh buffer.
- `Vt::new()` creates a non-resizable Vt. All Vts for playback must use `Vt::builder().size(w, h).resizable(true).scrollback_limit(0).build()`.
- `avt::Cursor` is not publicly importable — we define our own `CursorState` type.

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/snapshot.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs` and `Cargo.toml` (add `rgb = "0.8"` dependency)

## Tickets

- 1.1.1 — Snapshot types, capture, and restore
- 1.1.2 — Snapshot tests

title: 1.1.1 — Snapshot types, capture, and restore

- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` has deps `avt.workspace = true`, `serde.workspace = true`, `serde_json.workspace = true`. Dev-deps: `insta`, `tempfile`, `pretty_assertions` (all `.workspace = true`).
- Root `Cargo.toml` has `[workspace.dependencies]` with `avt = "0.11"`, `serde`, `serde_json`, etc. Add `rgb = "0.8"` in the same section.
- `snapshot.rs` does not exist yet — create it.

**Other epics may modify `lib.rs` concurrently.** Just add your `pub mod snapshot;` line and re-exports. Do not assume the file is empty or that you own it exclusively.

**Adding the `rgb` dependency — exact syntax:**
- Root `Cargo.toml`: add `rgb = "0.8"` under `[workspace.dependencies]`
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml`: add `rgb.workspace = true` under `[dependencies]`

## avt API quick reference

Key methods you will use (avt 0.11):

```
Vt::builder() -> Builder
Builder::size(cols: usize, rows: usize) -> &mut Builder
Builder::resizable(bool) -> &mut Builder
Builder::scrollback_limit(limit: usize) -> &mut Builder
Builder::build() -> Vt

vt.feed_str(s: &str) -> (Vec<usize>, bool)  // (dirty_lines, was_resized)
vt.dump() -> String          // serializes full state as escape sequences
vt.size() -> (usize, usize)  // (cols, rows) — NOTE: returns usize, not u16
vt.cursor() -> Cursor         // Cursor { pub col: usize, pub row: usize, pub visible: bool }
vt.view() -> &[Line]          // visible screen rows (exactly `rows` lines)
```

**Important**: `vt.size()` returns `(usize, usize)`. The snapshot stores `(u16, u16)`. You need an explicit cast: `width: size.0 as u16`.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.1.2 — Snapshot tests
description:
Comprehensive tests for snapshot capture/restore round-trips and cursor state extraction.

## Tests to implement

Add `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` to `snapshot.rs`:

1. **Basic round-trip**: Create Vt 10x4 via `create_vt(10, 4)`, feed `"hello\r\nworld"`, capture snapshot, restore to new Vt. Verify `view()` line text matches line-by-line (use `line.text()`). Verify `cursor()` col/row match between original and restored.

2. **Colors and attributes**: Feed escape sequences for bold (`\x1b[1m`), fg color (`\x1b[31m`), bg color (`\x1b[42m`), inverse (`\x1b[7m`). Write some text. Capture/restore. Verify attributes preserved by comparing `line.cells()` pen attributes (`is_bold()`, `foreground()`, etc.) on the restored Vt.

3. **Cursor state**: Test `CursorState::from_vt` across multiple scenarios in one test:
   - Default position (0, 0, visible) on a fresh Vt.
   - After feeding text to move cursor — verify col/row update.
   - Hide cursor (`\x1b[?25l`), capture/restore, verify `visible == false`.
   - Show cursor (`\x1b[?25h`), capture/restore, verify `visible == true`.

4. **Alternate buffer**: Enter alternate buffer (`\x1b[?1049h`), write text ("alt buffer content"), capture/restore, verify restored Vt shows the text via `view()`. Then in the same test or a follow-on assertion: exit alternate (`\x1b[?1049l`), write "primary content", capture/restore, verify primary buffer is correct. Add a comment explaining the asymmetry: alternate buffer content is intentionally not captured when primary is active — entering alternate always starts fresh.

5. **Resize**: Create Vt 80x24, feed resize escape sequence `\x1b[8;40;120t` (**note: xtwinops order is rows;cols**), write text. Capture snapshot. Assert `snapshot.width() == 120` and `snapshot.height() == 40`. Restore and verify `vt.size() == (120, 40)` and text content matches.

6. **Fresh Vt (empty recording edge case)**: Create Vt 80x24, capture immediately (no feed). Restore. Verify both are blank (all lines are empty/spaces) and cursors match (0, 0, visible).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core -- snapshot` passes
- [ ] All 6 test cases are implemented and pass
- [ ] Resize test uses correct xtwinops parameter order (rows;cols, not cols;rows)
- [ ] Alternate buffer asymmetry is documented with a comment in the test
- [ ] Fresh/empty Vt round-trip is tested
notes:
## avt API reference for tests

You will need these avt APIs to write assertions:

```
// Line text extraction
line.text() -> String              // plain text of a line

// Cell iteration (for attribute verification)
line.cells() -> impl Iterator<Item = (char, Pen)>

// Pen attribute getters
pen.foreground() -> Option<Color>  // Color::Indexed(u8) or Color::RGB(rgb::RGB8)
pen.background() -> Option<Color>
pen.is_bold() -> bool
pen.is_faint() -> bool
pen.is_italic() -> bool
pen.is_underline() -> bool
pen.is_strikethrough() -> bool
pen.is_blink() -> bool
pen.is_inverse() -> bool

// Color types
avt::Color::Indexed(u8)
avt::Color::RGB(rgb::RGB8)    // rgb::RGB8 has pub fields r, g, b
```

**Comparing restored state**: The simplest approach is to compare line text (`line.text()`) for content and spot-check specific cells for attributes. Do NOT try to compare `Line` directly (it has private fields).

**The testdata_path helper** is mentioned for convention only — this ticket builds all Vts in-memory. Do not import testdata files.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo test -p speedrun-core -- snapshot
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 0.2 — Tooling and pre-commit hooks
description:
Set up formatting, linting, and pre-commit hooks so that CI-equivalent checks run locally before every commit.

## Tasks

- Create `rustfmt.toml` at workspace root with default config (empty file — use rustfmt defaults).
- Add `[workspace.lints.clippy]` to root `Cargo.toml` with `all = "deny"`. Both crates inherit via `[lints] workspace = true`.
- Create a pre-commit hook script at `scripts/install-hooks.sh` that symlinks `.git/hooks/pre-commit` to a `scripts/pre-commit` script. The pre-commit script runs, in order:
  1. `cargo fmt --check` — reject unformatted code
  2. `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` — reject lint warnings
  3. `cargo test --workspace` — reject failing tests
  If any step fails, the commit is aborted with a clear message indicating which check failed.
- The install script should be idempotent (safe to run multiple times).
- Document in a comment at the top of `install-hooks.sh`: run `bash scripts/install-hooks.sh` after cloning.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `rustfmt.toml` exists at workspace root
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes on the scaffold
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes on the scaffold
- [ ] `scripts/pre-commit` exists and is executable
- [ ] `scripts/install-hooks.sh` exists and is executable
- [ ] After running `install-hooks.sh`, a commit with a fmt violation (e.g., extra spaces) is rejected by the hook
- [ ] After running `install-hooks.sh`, a commit with a clippy warning is rejected by the hook
- [ ] Running `install-hooks.sh` twice does not error or create duplicate hooks

title: 0.3 — Test data
description:
Create synthetic .cast files for testing. All files are hand-crafted, minimal, and human-readable. These are the fixtures that Phase 1 parser tests will validate against.

## Asciicast format reference

**V2 header:** First line is a JSON object. Required fields: `version` (int, must be 2), `width` (int), `height` (int). Optional: `timestamp` (unix epoch), `idle_time_limit` (float, seconds), `title` (string), `env` (object).
Example: `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24}`

**V2 events:** Subsequent lines are JSON arrays: `[timestamp, "event_type", "data"]`.
Timestamps are absolute seconds from start (float). Event types: `o` (output), `i` (input), `m` (marker with label as data), `r` (resize, data is `"COLSxROWS"`).
Example: `[0.5, "o", "hello\r\n"]`

**V3 header:** Same as V2 but `"version": 3`.
**V3 events:** Same array shape but timestamps are relative (interval since previous event, not absolute).

## Valid test files (testdata/)

- `minimal_v2.cast`: V2 header (80x24) + 3-5 output events with varying timestamps (e.g., 0.5s, 1.2s, 2.0s). Content: simple text like `$ hello\r\nworld\r\n`.
- `minimal_v3.cast`: V3 header (80x24) + 3-5 output events with relative timestamps (e.g., 0.5, 0.7, 0.8). Same kind of simple output.
- `empty.cast`: Valid V2 header, zero event lines.
- `with_markers.cast`: V2 header + mix of output and at least 2 marker (`m`) events with labels. E.g., `[3.0, "m", "chapter-1"]`, `[7.0, "m", "chapter-2"]`.
- `with_resize.cast`: V2 header (80x24) + output events + at least 1 resize (`r`) event. E.g., `[2.0, "r", "120x40"]`.
- `long_idle.cast`: V2 header with `"idle_time_limit": 2`. Events: output at 1.0s, then output at 35.0s (34-second gap), then output at 36.0s. This tests idle capping.
- `alternate_buffer.cast`: V2 header + output events that switch to alternate screen buffer and back. Use these exact sequences:
  - Enter alt buffer: `\u001b[?1049h`
  - Write something in alt buffer
  - Exit alt buffer: `\u001b[?1049l`
- `real_session.cast`: A realistic recording, 30+ seconds long. Hand-craft ~20 output events over 30s simulating a shell session (commands, output, prompts with realistic timing gaps).

## Invalid test files (testdata/invalid/)

- `empty_file.cast`: Exactly zero bytes.
- `no_header.cast`: Event lines only, no JSON header on line 1. E.g., `[0.5, "o", "hello"]` as first line.
- `bad_json.cast`: Invalid JSON on line 1. E.g., `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24` (missing closing brace).
- `bad_version.cast`: Valid JSON header but `"version": 99`.
- `missing_fields.cast`: Valid JSON header but missing `width` and `height`. E.g., `{"version": 2}`.
- `bad_event.cast`: Valid header followed by malformed events: `[0.5, "o"]` (missing data), `not json at all`, `[null, "o", "x"]` (null timestamp).
- `binary_garbage.cast`: 64 bytes of random binary content (non-UTF8).
- `truncated.cast`: Valid header + one valid event + one incomplete line (e.g., `[1.5, "o", "hel`).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All valid .cast files exist in `testdata/`
- [ ] All invalid .cast files exist in `testdata/invalid/`
- [ ] Each valid file's first line is parseable JSON with the correct `version` field
- [ ] `long_idle.cast` header has `"idle_time_limit": 2`
- [ ] `with_markers.cast` contains at least 2 lines with `"m"` event type
- [ ] `with_resize.cast` contains at least 1 line with `"r"` event type
- [ ] `alternate_buffer.cast` contains the `1049h` and `1049l` escape sequences
- [ ] `empty_file.cast` is exactly 0 bytes
- [ ] `binary_garbage.cast` is not valid UTF-8
- [ ] File count: 8 valid + 8 invalid = 16 total .cast files

title: Epic 1.3 — Time Mapper
description:
Apply idle time limits to produce effective timestamps for each event. Computed once at load time.

## Context

Recordings may contain long idle gaps (e.g., 30+ seconds of inactivity). The idle limit (from CLI flag, header, or none) caps these gaps to produce a compressed "effective" timeline. All public API surfaces (progress bar, seek, duration) use effective time.

### Key design decisions

- **Decoupled from parser types**: `TimeMap::build()` takes a `&[f64]` of raw timestamps, not `&[Event]`. The caller extracts timestamps.
- **Idle limit as `Option<f64>`**: `None` = no capping (effective = raw). No enum wrapper.
- **First event time preserved**: the gap between t=0 and the first event is NOT capped. Only inter-event gaps are subject to idle limiting.
- **Binary search for seeking**: `event_index_at(time)` returns `Option<usize>` — the last event at or before the given effective time. Returns `None` for empty maps or times before the first event.

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/timemap.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.3.1 — TimeMap implementation
- 1.3.2 — TimeMap tests

title: 1.3.1 — TimeMap implementation
description:
Implement the time mapper that converts raw event timestamps to effective (idle-capped) timestamps.

## Cross-cutting conventions

- **Idle limit as `Option<f64>`**: `None` = no capping. No enum wrapper.
- **Decoupled from parser**: `TimeMap::build()` takes `&[f64]` (raw timestamps), NOT `&[Event]`. The caller extracts timestamps. This keeps the module independent for parallel development.
- **Derives**: `TimeMap`: `Debug`. `TimeMapError`: `Debug`.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/timemap.rs` with:

   ```rust
   /// Mapping from raw timestamps to effective (idle-capped) timestamps.
   /// Computed once at load time. One entry per event, parallel to the events vec.
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub struct TimeMap {
       effective_times: Vec<f64>,
       duration: f64,
   }

   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum TimeMapError {
       /// Idle limit must be positive.
       InvalidIdleLimit(f64),
   }
   ```
   Implement `std::fmt::Display` and `std::error::Error` for `TimeMapError`.

2. **Construction**:
   ```rust
   impl TimeMap {
       /// Build a time map from raw event timestamps.
       ///
       /// `idle_limit`: if `Some(limit)`, inter-event gaps exceeding `limit`
       /// seconds are capped to `limit`. If `None`, effective = raw.
       pub fn build(raw_times: &[f64], idle_limit: Option<f64>) -> Result<Self, TimeMapError>
   }
   ```

3. **Algorithm**:
   - If `idle_limit` is `Some(limit)` and `limit <= 0.0` → return `Err(InvalidIdleLimit(limit))`.
   - If `raw_times` is empty → return `Ok(TimeMap { effective_times: vec![], duration: 0.0 })`.
   - `effective_times[0] = raw_times[0]`. The gap between t=0 and the first event is NOT capped — it is the recording's natural start offset.
   - For `i` in `1..len`:
     - `gap = raw_times[i] - raw_times[i-1]`
     - `capped_gap = match idle_limit { Some(limit) => gap.min(limit), None => gap }`
     - `effective_times[i] = effective_times[i-1] + capped_gap`
   - `duration = *effective_times.last().unwrap_or(&0.0)`

4. **Accessors**:
   ```rust
   /// Effective timestamp for event at index. None if out of bounds.
   pub fn effective_time(&self, index: usize) -> Option<f64>

   /// Total effective duration (effective time of last event, or 0.0 if empty).
   pub fn duration(&self) -> f64

   /// Number of entries (same as number of events).
   pub fn len(&self) -> usize
   pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool

   /// Find the last event at or before `time` in effective time.
   /// Returns None if the time map is empty or time is before the first event.
   pub fn event_index_at(&self, time: f64) -> Option<usize>
   ```

5. **`event_index_at` implementation**: Use `partition_point` for binary search on `effective_times`. Find the number of entries where `effective_times[i] <= time`, then return `that_count - 1` (the last entry at-or-before). If the count is 0 (time is before all entries), return `None`. If the vec is empty, return `None`.

   Note on f64 precision: the values produced by this algorithm are simple additions/subtractions of small numbers. No epsilon tolerance is needed — exact `<=` comparison is correct for the values we produce.

6. Add `pub mod timemap;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use timemap::{TimeMap, TimeMapError};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo build -p speedrun-core` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `TimeMap::build` handles: no limit, with limit, empty input, invalid limit (<=0)
- [ ] First event time is preserved (not capped)
- [ ] Inter-event gaps exceeding the limit are capped; gaps at or below limit are untouched
- [ ] `event_index_at` returns `None` for empty maps and times before first event
- [ ] `event_index_at` returns correct index for exact matches, between-events, and past-end
- [ ] `TimeMapError` implements `Display` and `Error`
- [ ] Types are exported from `lib.rs`
notes:
## Codebase context

**Current state of files you will modify:**

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/lib.rs` contains only: `// speedrun-core library - Terminal session player core engine` (1 line, no modules).
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` already has all needed deps. No new dependencies required for this module.
- `timemap.rs` does not exist yet — create it.

**Other epics may modify `lib.rs` concurrently** (snapshot.rs and parser.rs are being built in parallel). Just add your `pub mod timemap;` line and re-exports. Do not assume the file is empty or that you own it exclusively.

**This module has no dependency on the parser module.** `TimeMap::build()` takes `&[f64]` (raw timestamps), not `&[Event]`. This is deliberate — it keeps the epics independent for parallel development.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.3.2 — TimeMap tests
description:
Tests for the time mapper. Tests use raw timestamp slices directly — no dependency on the parser module.

## Tests to implement

Add `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` to `timemap.rs`:

### Core behavior

1. **Idle limit capping**: Raw times `[1.0, 1.1, 35.0, 35.1, 36.0]`. Test three scenarios in one test (or three closely related tests):
   - `idle_limit: Some(2.0)` → effective `[1.0, 1.1, 3.1, 3.2, 4.1]`, duration `4.1`
   - `idle_limit: Some(1.0)` → effective `[1.0, 1.1, 2.1, 2.2, 3.1]`, duration `3.1`
   - `idle_limit: None` → effective equals raw, duration `36.0`

2. **Empty event list**: `raw_times: &[]`, `idle_limit: Some(2.0)`. Duration `0.0`, len `0`, is_empty `true`.

3. **Single event**: Raw `[5.0]`, `idle_limit: Some(2.0)`. Effective `[5.0]`, duration `5.0`.

4. **Multiple consecutive idle gaps**: Raw `[0.0, 1.0, 20.0, 21.0, 40.0]`, limit `Some(2.0)`. Two gaps capped. Expected effective: `[0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0]`, duration `6.0`.

5. **First event at t>0**: Raw `[5.0, 6.0, 7.0]`, limit `Some(2.0)`. First event time 5.0 is preserved (not capped). Expected: `[5.0, 6.0, 7.0]`, duration `7.0`.

### Error cases

6. **Invalid idle limits**: `Some(0.0)` and `Some(-1.0)` both → `TimeMapError::InvalidIdleLimit`. Can be one test with two assertions.

### `event_index_at` tests

7. **Binary search correctness**: Build a TimeMap from test 1 (effective times `[1.0, 1.1, 3.1, 3.2, 4.1]`). Verify:
   - `event_index_at(0.5)` → `None` (before first)
   - `event_index_at(1.0)` → `Some(0)` (exact first)
   - `event_index_at(1.05)` → `Some(0)` (between)
   - `event_index_at(2.0)` → `Some(1)` (between)
   - `event_index_at(4.1)` → `Some(4)` (exact last)
   - `event_index_at(100.0)` → `Some(4)` (past end)
   - Empty map: `event_index_at(0.0)` → `None`
   - Single-event map (`[5.0]`): `event_index_at(4.0)` → `None`, `event_index_at(5.0)` → `Some(0)`

### Insta snapshot

8. Snapshot the effective times for the long_idle scenario (test 1, limit 2.0). Use `insta::assert_debug_snapshot!`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core -- timemap` passes
- [ ] All 8 test cases are implemented and pass
- [ ] Edge cases for empty, single-event, before-first-event, and past-end are covered
- [ ] Error cases for invalid idle limits are tested
- [ ] Insta snapshot captures the effective times
notes:
## Codebase context

Ticket 1.3.1 will have created `timemap.rs` with the full implementation. You are adding tests to this file.

## Snapshotting private fields

The `effective_times` field on `TimeMap` is private. For the insta snapshot test (#14), build a vec from the public accessor:

```rust

    .map(|i| tm.effective_time(i).unwrap())
    .collect();
insta::assert_debug_snapshot!(times);
```

Do NOT make `effective_times` public. Use the accessor method.

## insta usage

`insta` is already in dev-dependencies. First run creates snapshot files in `crates/speedrun-core/src/snapshots/`. Run `cargo insta review` to accept (or set `INSTA_UPDATE=always`).


title: Epic 1.2 — Asciicast Parser
description:
Parse asciicast v2 and v3 files into an in-memory event list with typed events, eager resize parsing, and strict error handling.

## Context

The parser reads from `impl Read` (wrapped in `BufReader` internally). V2 timestamps are absolute; V3 timestamps are relative intervals normalized to absolute during parsing.

### Key design decisions

- **Strict error handling**: fails on first malformed event line. Lenient/skip mode deferred to Epic 4.1.
- **Eager resize parsing**: `"r"` events with `"COLSxROWS"` data are parsed into `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }` at parse time — no stringly-typed data passed downstream.
- **Monotonicity validation**: V2 timestamps must be non-decreasing, V3 intervals must be non-negative.
- **Markers extracted twice**: present in the events vec (for replay ordering) AND in a separate `markers: Vec<Marker>` (for player navigation API).

### Error types

- `EmptyFile` — zero bytes / no lines
- `InvalidHeader` — first line isn't valid JSON header (with source error)
- `UnsupportedVersion` — version not 2 or 3
- `MissingField` — header missing width/height
- `InvalidEvent` — malformed event line (with line number)
- `InvalidResize` — bad `"COLSxROWS"` format
- `NotUtf8` / `Io` — I/O and encoding errors

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.2.1 — Parser types and error handling
- 1.2.2 — Parse implementation
- 1.2.3 — Parser tests
notes:
## Update: Unknown event types are skipped, not errored

Analysis of a real v3 recording revealed event type `"x"` (exit) which is not in the o/i/m/r set. The parser now **skips unknown event types silently** rather than erroring. This ensures real-world recordings parse successfully.

Also: V3 headers use `term.cols`/`term.rows` instead of top-level `width`/`height`. The `RawHeader` serde struct handles both formats.

title: 1.2.1 — Parser types and error handling
description:
Define all parser types, the error enum, and wire the module into lib.rs. No parsing logic yet.

## Cross-cutting conventions

- **Derives**: `Header`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Event`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `EventType`: `Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize`. `EventData`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Marker`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Recording`: `Debug, Serialize`.
- **`impl Read` API**: The public `parse()` function signature accepts `impl Read`, wraps in `BufReader` internally. Declare the signature as a stub (returning `todo!()`) so the API shape is visible.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs` with types:

   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
   pub struct Recording {
       pub header: Header,
       pub events: Vec<Event>,
       pub markers: Vec<Marker>,  // Extracted from events for convenience
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Header {
       pub version: u8,           // 2 or 3
       pub width: u16,
       pub height: u16,
       pub timestamp: Option<u64>,
       pub idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
       pub title: Option<String>,
       pub env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]
   pub enum EventType {
       Output,
       Input,
       Marker,
       Resize,
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Event {
       pub time: f64,              // Raw absolute timestamp (seconds)
       pub event_type: EventType,
       pub data: EventData,
   }

   /// Typed event data. Resize is eagerly parsed into structured dimensions
   /// so downstream consumers never parse "COLSxROWS" strings.
   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub enum EventData {
       Text(String),
       Resize { cols: u16, rows: u16 },
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Marker {
       pub time: f64,    // Raw absolute timestamp (effective time from TimeMap)
       pub label: String,
   }
   ```

2. Define `ParseError`:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum ParseError {
       EmptyFile,
       InvalidHeader { line: String, source: serde_json::Error },
       UnsupportedVersion { version: u64 },
       MissingField { field: &'static str },
       InvalidEvent { line_number: usize, content: String, reason: String },
       InvalidResize { line_number: usize, data: String },
       NotUtf8 { source: std::io::Error },
       Io { source: std::io::Error },
   }
   ```
   Implement `std::fmt::Display` and `std::error::Error` for `ParseError`.

3. Declare a private serde-deserialization struct for the raw header:
   ```rust

   struct RawHeader {
       version: u64,              // u64 to detect unsupported versions like 99
       width: Option<u16>,
       height: Option<u16>,
       timestamp: Option<u64>,
       idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
       title: Option<String>,
       env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   }
   ```

4. Declare the public API stub:
   ```rust
   pub fn parse(reader: impl std::io::Read) -> Result<Recording, ParseError> {
       todo!()
   }
   ```

5. Add `pub mod parser;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export key types: `pub use parser::{Recording, Header, Event, EventType, EventData, Marker, ParseError, parse};`

- [ ] All types have the specified derives
- [ ] `ParseError` implements `Display` and `Error`
- [ ] `parse()` stub exists with the correct signature

**Serde usage**: The workspace declares `serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }`, so `#[derive(Serialize)]` works with just `use serde::Serialize;`. For `Deserialize` (used on `RawHeader`): `use serde::Deserialize;`.

**The `todo!()` stub** is intentional — the implementation comes in ticket 1.2.2. The stub lets the types compile and be visible in the API while deferring logic. `cargo test` will not call `parse()` so the `todo!()` will not panic.

## IMPORTANT: V3 header format differs from V2

The real v3 asciicast format nests dimensions under `term`:

**V2 header**: `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24, ...}`
**V3 header**: `{"version": 3, "term": {"cols": 188, "rows": 50, "type": "xterm-ghostty", ...}, ...}`

The `RawHeader` serde struct must handle both. Recommended approach:

```rust
#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct RawHeader {
    version: u64,
    // V2 fields (top-level)
    width: Option<u16>,
    height: Option<u16>,
    // V3 fields (nested under term)
    term: Option<RawTerm>,
    // Shared
    timestamp: Option<u64>,
    idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
    title: Option<String>,
    env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct RawTerm {
    cols: Option<u16>,
    rows: Option<u16>,
    #[serde(rename = "type")]
    term_type: Option<String>,
    version: Option<String>,
    // ignore theme and other fields
}
```

When converting to public `Header`:
- V2: use `width`/`height` directly
- V3: use `term.cols`/`term.rows`
- Validation: after version check, verify the appropriate fields are present. MissingField error should say "width" (v2) or "term.cols" (v3) depending on version.

title: 1.2.2 — Parse implementation
description:
Implement the `parse()` function: header parsing, event parsing, V3 normalization, resize eager parsing, monotonicity validation.

## Tasks

1. **`parse()` entry point**: Accept `impl Read`, wrap in `BufReader`, read lines, delegate to header and event parsing helpers.

2. **Header parsing**:
   - Read first line. If EOF → `ParseError::EmptyFile`.
   - Binary garbage detection: if the first line contains non-UTF8 bytes, or if the first 32 bytes contain non-printable characters (excluding \t, \n, \r), return an early error (`NotUtf8` or `InvalidHeader`).
   - Deserialize via serde into `RawHeader` (the private struct from 1.2.1). On failure → `InvalidHeader { line, source }`.
   - Validate `version` is 2 or 3 → else `UnsupportedVersion { version }`.
   - Validate `width` is present → else `MissingField { field: "width" }`.
   - Validate `height` is present → else `MissingField { field: "height" }`.
   - Convert `RawHeader` to public `Header` (narrow version from u64 to u8).

3. **Event parsing**:
   - Read subsequent lines. Track line numbers (starting from 1 for the header).
   - Skip empty/whitespace-only lines gracefully.
   - Parse each line as `serde_json::Value`. If not valid JSON → `InvalidEvent { line_number, content, reason: "invalid JSON" }`.
   - Expect a JSON array of exactly 3 elements → else `InvalidEvent` with reason "expected array of 3 elements".
   - Extract timestamp: must be a JSON number, non-null. Convert to f64. If null or non-numeric → `InvalidEvent` with reason "invalid timestamp".
   - Validate timestamp/interval is non-negative → else `InvalidEvent` with reason "negative timestamp".
   - Extract event type string: map `"o"→Output`, `"i"→Input`, `"m"→Marker`, `"r"→Resize`. Unknown type → `InvalidEvent` with reason "unknown event type: X".
   - Extract data: must be a JSON string → else `InvalidEvent`.

4. **V3 timestamp normalization**: If `header.version == 3`, timestamps are relative intervals. Accumulate absolute time:
   ```
   absolute_time = 0.0
   for each event:
       absolute_time += interval
       event.time = absolute_time
   ```
   The first event's interval is relative to t=0.

5. **Monotonicity check (V2 only)**: For version 2, verify each event's timestamp >= the previous event's timestamp. If not → `InvalidEvent { line_number, content, reason: "timestamps must be non-decreasing" }`. (V3 intervals are already validated as non-negative in step 3.)

6. **Resize eager parsing**: If event type is `Resize`, parse the data string:
   - Split on `'x'`. Expect exactly 2 parts.
   - Parse each part as `u16`.
   - On failure → `InvalidResize { line_number, data }`.
   - Store as `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`.
   - **Note**: the data format is `"COLSxROWS"` (cols first, rows second). The xtwinops escape sequence uses the opposite order (`rows;cols`) — but that's the indexer's concern, not the parser's. The parser stores the parsed values by name.

7. **Marker extraction**: If event type is `Marker`, push `Marker { time, label: data_string }` to the markers vec. The event also goes into the events vec with `EventData::Text(label)`.

8. **All other events**: Store `EventData::Text(data_string)`.

9. Return `Recording { header, events, markers }`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `parse()` works end-to-end for all 8 valid testdata files (verify in next ticket)
- [ ] V3 timestamps are normalized to absolute
- [ ] Resize data is eagerly parsed into `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`
- [ ] Markers appear in both `events` vec and `markers` vec
- [ ] V2 non-monotonic timestamps produce `InvalidEvent`
- [ ] V3 negative intervals produce `InvalidEvent`
- [ ] Empty/whitespace-only lines are skipped
- [ ] Line numbers in errors are correct (1-indexed, header is line 1)
- [ ] Strict mode: first malformed event line stops parsing and returns error
notes:
## Codebase context

Ticket 1.2.1 will have already created `parser.rs` with all types, `ParseError`, `RawHeader`, and a `parse()` stub returning `todo!()`. You are replacing the `todo!()` with the real implementation.

## UTF-8 and binary garbage handling — clarification

`BufReader::lines()` returns `io::Result<String>`. If the underlying bytes are not valid UTF-8, `lines()` returns `Err(io::Error { kind: InvalidData, ... })`. This means:

- **You do NOT need to pre-read raw bytes** for binary garbage detection. Just attempt to read the first line via `lines().next()`. If it returns `Err(e)` with `e.kind() == ErrorKind::InvalidData`, map it to `ParseError::NotUtf8 { source: e }`.
- If it returns `Ok(line)`, proceed with JSON parsing.
- The `binary_garbage.cast` test file will trigger this path naturally.

So the actual flow is:
```rust
let reader = BufReader::new(reader);
let mut lines = reader.lines();
let first_line = match lines.next() {
    None => return Err(ParseError::EmptyFile),
    Some(Err(e)) => /* check e.kind() for InvalidData → NotUtf8, else Io */,
    Some(Ok(line)) => line,
};
// Now parse `first_line` as header JSON...
```

## Verification commands

After implementing, smoke-test with at least one file:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# Quick smoke test (will be fully tested in 1.2.3):
cargo test -p speedrun-core
```
## IMPORTANT: Two changes from real-world recording analysis

### 1. V3 header parsing

V3 headers nest dimensions under `term`:
```json
{"version": 3, "term": {"cols": 188, "rows": 50, "type": "xterm-ghostty"}, "timestamp": 1772729753, "env": {"SHELL": "/bin/zsh"}}
```

When parsing the header, after detecting version:
- **V2**: read `width` and `height` from top level
- **V3**: read `term.cols` and `term.rows` from nested `term` object

Both produce the same `Header` struct (which has `width` and `height` fields).

### 2. Unknown event types are SKIPPED, not errored

Real-world v3 recordings contain event types like `"x"` (exit) that our parser does not handle. The parser should **skip unknown event types silently** rather than returning `InvalidEvent`. This is a pragmatic choice — real recordings must parse successfully.

Change from the original plan:
- **Before**: Unknown event type → `InvalidEvent` with reason "unknown event type: X"
- **After**: Unknown event type → skip the event, do not add to events vec, continue parsing

The synthetic "unknown event type" test in 1.2.3 should verify the event is skipped and parsing continues (not that it errors).

title: Epic 1.4 — Keyframe Indexer
description:
Build the keyframe index that enables instant seeking. Replay all events through `avt`, capturing `TerminalSnapshot`s every 5 seconds of effective time. The resulting `KeyframeIndex` supports O(log n) lookup for the seek engine.

## Context

The indexer is the bridge between parsing/time-mapping (done) and seeking/playback (next). It depends on all three completed modules:
- `parser` — `Recording`, `Event`, `EventType`, `EventData`
- `timemap` — `TimeMap` for effective timestamps
- `snapshot` — `TerminalSnapshot`, `create_vt`

### Key design decisions

- `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL = 5.0` seconds of effective time (constant, not configurable for v1).
- Always capture a "keyframe 0" at effective time 0 (initial terminal state before any events), so seeking to start is instant. If the recording has zero events, the index is empty.
- Only output events feed to the Vt via `feed_str()`. Input and marker events are skipped (no terminal state change).
- Resize events resize the Vt by feeding the xtwinops escape sequence `\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t`. **Note the parameter order**: rows then cols — opposite of `EventData::Resize` which stores `{ cols, rows }`. See `snapshot.rs:240` for the validated pattern.
- `Keyframe.event_index` = index of the first event NOT included in the snapshot (i.e., the first event to replay after restoring this keyframe).

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.4.1 — Keyframe types and index builder
- 1.4.2 — Keyframe index tests

title: 1.4.1 — Keyframe types and index builder
description:
Define the keyframe types and implement the index builder that replays events through avt, capturing snapshots at 5-second effective-time intervals.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` with types:

   ```rust
   /// Interval between keyframes in effective time (seconds).
   pub const KEYFRAME_INTERVAL: f64 = 5.0;

   /// A snapshot of terminal state at a specific effective time.
   #[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   pub struct Keyframe {
       /// Effective timestamp (with idle limit applied).
       pub time: f64,
       /// Index of the first event NOT included in this snapshot.
       /// To resume playback from this keyframe, start replaying from
       /// `events[event_index]`.
       pub event_index: usize,
       /// Serialized terminal state.
       pub snapshot: TerminalSnapshot,
   }

   /// Index of keyframes for O(log n) seek lookup.
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub struct KeyframeIndex {
       keyframes: Vec<Keyframe>,
   }
   ```

2. Implement `KeyframeIndex::build(recording: &Recording, time_map: &TimeMap) -> Self`:

   - If the recording has no events, return an empty index.
   - Create a `Vt` at header dimensions via `create_vt(width as usize, height as usize)`.
   - Capture the initial keyframe ("keyframe 0"): **`time = 0.0`** (the start of the timeline, before any events), `event_index = 0`, snapshot of the fresh Vt.
   - Set `next_keyframe_time = 0.0 + KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` (i.e., 5.0).
   - Iterate through all events by index `i`:
     - **Before processing** the event, check if `effective_time(i) >= next_keyframe_time`. If so, capture a keyframe with the current Vt state, `event_index = i`, `time = effective_time(i)`. Then **advance `next_keyframe_time` past the current event** to avoid double-captures on subsequent events:
       ```rust
       while next_keyframe_time <= effective_time {
           next_keyframe_time += KEYFRAME_INTERVAL;
       }
       ```
       This handles gaps spanning multiple intervals (e.g., a 17s gap should produce one keyframe, not three).
     - `EventType::Output` with `EventData::Text(data)`: call `vt.feed_str(&data)`.
     - `EventType::Resize` with `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`: feed `format!("\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t")` to the Vt. **Critical**: the xtwinops escape sequence takes rows then cols — the opposite order from `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`.
     - `EventType::Input` and `EventType::Marker`: skip (no terminal state change).

3. Implement `KeyframeIndex::keyframe_at(time: f64) -> Option<usize>`:

   - Binary search for the last keyframe with `keyframe.time <= time`.
   - Use `partition_point` (same pattern as `TimeMap::event_index_at`).
   - Return the index into the keyframes vec, or `None` if empty or time is before the first keyframe.

4. Implement accessors:

   - `get(index: usize) -> Option<&Keyframe>`
   - `len() -> usize`
   - `is_empty() -> bool`

5. Add `pub mod index;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use index::{Keyframe, KeyframeIndex, KEYFRAME_INTERVAL};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `Keyframe` struct with `time`, `event_index`, `snapshot` fields
- [ ] `KeyframeIndex` wraps `Vec<Keyframe>` with build/query methods
- [ ] `build()` replays events through avt, captures snapshots at correct effective-time intervals
- [ ] Initial keyframe has `time = 0.0` for non-empty recordings; empty recordings produce empty index
- [ ] Output events feed to Vt, resize events resize Vt via xtwinops (rows,cols order), input/marker skipped
- [ ] Large time gaps produce a single keyframe (not backfilled), and `next_keyframe_time` advances correctly past the gap
- [ ] `keyframe_at()` returns correct index via binary search
- [ ] Module added to `lib.rs` with re-exports
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes

title: 1.4.2 — Keyframe index tests
description:
Comprehensive tests for the keyframe index builder and lookup.

## Tasks

1. **Empty recording** (header only, no events): Index should be empty (`len() == 0`, `is_empty() == true`).

2. **Short recording (< 5s effective)**: Use `minimal_v2.cast`. Only the initial keyframe should exist (keyframe 0). Verify its `time == 0.0`, `event_index == 0`, and snapshot is the initial empty terminal.

3. **Multiple keyframe intervals**: Construct a synthetic `Recording` with events spanning >15s of effective time (e.g., output events at 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15s). Build with `TimeMap`. Verify correct number of keyframes, correct `time` values, and correct `event_index` values.

4. **Idle-capped recording**: Load `long_idle.cast` (has `idle_time_limit: 2`, raw events at ~[0, 1, 35, 35.1, 36]). Build TimeMap with the header's idle limit. Effective duration is ~4.1s — less than one keyframe interval. Verify only the initial keyframe exists. This confirms keyframes align to effective time, not raw time.

5. **Smoke test with all test data files**: Build the index from each test data file (`minimal_v2.cast`, `minimal_v3.cast`, `empty.cast`, `long_idle.cast`, `with_markers.cast`, `with_resize.cast`, `alternate_buffer.cast`, `real_session.cast`). Verify each builds without panic and produces a reasonable keyframe count. This is a single test function with a loop — not individual tests per file.

6. **`keyframe_at()` lookup correctness**: Using the multi-interval index from task 3:
   - Exact keyframe time → returns that keyframe's index
   - Time between two keyframes → returns the earlier keyframe's index
   - Time before first keyframe → returns `None`
   - Time at or past last keyframe → returns last keyframe's index
   - Empty index → returns `None`

7. **Insta snapshot test**: For the synthetic multi-interval recording, snapshot the vec of `(time, event_index)` tuples from all keyframes. This locks down the keyframe placement algorithm.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 7 test scenarios pass
- [ ] Tests use existing test data files where possible, synthetic recordings for precise timing control
- [ ] `keyframe_at()` edge cases comprehensively covered
- [ ] At least one insta snapshot test
- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core` all green

title: Epic 2.1 — CLI and Application Bootstrap
description:
Set up the CLI interface, terminal lifecycle management, and application skeleton for the TUI player. This is the entry point and process lifecycle — everything else in the TUI hangs on this.

## Context

Phase 1 (speedrun-core) is complete. The core `Player` API provides `load()`, `load_with()`, `seek()`, `tick()`, and all playback control. This epic wires that API into a running binary with proper terminal management.

### Key design decisions

- **clap derive API** for CLI parsing — consistent with Rust ecosystem conventions.
- **Terminal lifecycle** uses crossterm's raw mode + alternate screen. A panic hook ensures the terminal is always restored.
- **App struct** owns the Player and display state. The event loop is event-driven with deadline (no fixed tick rate).
- **Playback starts immediately** on launch (like `asciinema play`).
- `--start-at` seeks to position after load, then starts playing.

### Dependencies

- `speedrun-core` (path dep) — `Player`, `LoadOptions`, `PlayerError`
- `clap` (derive) — CLI argument parsing
- `crossterm` — raw mode, alternate screen, event polling
- `ratatui` — Terminal type (for the skeleton loop; real rendering is Epic 2.2)

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — rewrite from stub to full entry point
- `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` — new: App struct + event loop

## Tickets

- 2.1.1 — CLI parsing with clap
- 2.1.2 — Terminal lifecycle and panic hook
- 2.1.3 — App struct and skeleton event loop

title: 2.1.2 — Terminal lifecycle and panic hook
notes:
## ratatui 0.26 API note

In ratatui 0.26.3, the `Frame` struct has `fn size(&self) -> Rect`, NOT `fn area()`. The `area()` method was introduced in a later version. Use `frame.size()` everywhere.

The placeholder `run()` function should use:
```rust
let area = frame.size();  // NOT frame.area()
```

## Ctrl-C handling

crossterm in raw mode intercepts Ctrl-C. It arrives as a `KeyEvent` with `KeyCode::Char('c')` and `KeyModifiers::CONTROL`. The placeholder `run()` function should handle it:

```rust
if matches!(key.code, KeyCode::Char('q') | KeyCode::Esc)
    || (key.code == KeyCode::Char('c') && key.modifiers.contains(KeyModifiers::CONTROL))
{
    break;
}
```

Without this, Ctrl-C won't work and the user will be stuck in raw mode.

## `player` parameter usage

The `run()` placeholder function accepts `player` and `args` parameters but doesn't use them yet. Prefix with underscore to avoid clippy warnings: `_player: Player, _args: &Args`.

title: 2.1.3 — App struct and skeleton event loop
Epic 1.5 — Player
description:
The main public API for speedrun-core. The Player owns the Recording, TimeMap, KeyframeIndex, and current Vt instance. It provides loading, seeking, continuous playback, and single-step navigation.

## Context

This is the capstone of the core engine — it integrates all previous modules into a single API that the TUI (and future WASM player) consumes. The spec defines the full public API surface in SPEC.md lines 169–248.

### Key design decisions

- **`PlayerError`** wraps `ParseError` and `TimeMapError` (the two fallible operations during loading).
- **Idle limit resolution** precedence: `LoadOptions.idle_limit` > header `idle_time_limit` > no limit.
- The Player owns a `Vt` instance that is **rebuilt on every seek** (restored from keyframe snapshot + event replay) and **mutated in-place during tick/step**.
- **`screen()` returns `&[avt::Line]`** (via `vt.view()`). The spec says `&avt::Screen` but avt does not export a `Screen` type; `&[Line]` is the actual API.
- **Marker times** are converted from raw to effective at load time.
- **`step_backward()`** is internally a seek (avt is forward-only, so we must replay from a keyframe).
- **Auto-pause at end**: `tick()` returns `true` once for the final frame, `false` on subsequent calls.

### avt API surface (verified)

- `Vt::view() -> &[Line]` — line grid for rendering
- `Vt::cursor() -> Cursor` — cursor with `col`, `row`, `visible` fields
- `Vt::size() -> (usize, usize)` — (cols, rows)
- `Vt::feed_str(&mut self, s: &str) -> (Vec<usize>, bool)` — feed data, returns dirty lines (ignored)
- `Vt::dump() -> String` / `TerminalSnapshot::capture/restore` — state serialization (used by keyframe index)

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.5.1 — Player struct, load, seek, and screen access
- 1.5.2 — Playback control, tick, and stepping
- 1.5.3 — Player tests

title: 1.5.1 — Player struct, load, seek, and screen access
description:
Define the Player struct, error types, load pipeline, seek engine, and state accessors. This is the foundation that all playback control builds on.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` with error type:

   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum PlayerError {
       Parse(ParseError),
       TimeMap(TimeMapError),
   }
   ```

   Implement `std::fmt::Display`, `std::error::Error`, `From<ParseError>`, and `From<TimeMapError>`.

2. Define `LoadOptions`:

   ```rust
   pub struct LoadOptions {
       /// Cap idle time between events (seconds).
       /// `Some(limit)` overrides the header's `idle_time_limit`.
       /// `None` means use the header value, or no limit if the header
       /// doesn't specify one either.
       pub idle_limit: Option<f64>,
   }

   impl Default for LoadOptions {
       fn default() -> Self { Self { idle_limit: None } }
   }
   ```

3. Define `Player` struct (fields are private):

   ```rust
   pub struct Player {
       recording: Recording,
       time_map: TimeMap,
       index: KeyframeIndex,
       vt: avt::Vt,
       /// Current position in effective time.
       current_time: f64,
       /// Index of the next event to process (into recording.events).
       current_event_index: usize,
       /// Markers with effective times (converted at load).
       markers: Vec<Marker>,
       /// Whether playback is active.
       playing: bool,
       /// Playback speed multiplier.
       speed: f64,
   }
   ```

4. Implement `Player::load(reader: impl Read) -> Result<Self, PlayerError>`:
   - Delegates to `load_with(reader, LoadOptions::default())`.

5. Implement `Player::load_with(reader: impl Read, opts: LoadOptions) -> Result<Self, PlayerError>`:
   - Parse recording: `parse(reader)?`
   - Resolve idle limit: `opts.idle_limit.or(recording.header.idle_time_limit)` — CLI override > header > None (no limit).
   - Extract raw timestamps: `recording.events.iter().map(|e| e.time).collect()`
   - Build time map: `TimeMap::build(&raw_times, resolved_limit)?`
   - Build keyframe index: `KeyframeIndex::build(&recording, &time_map)`
   - Create initial Vt: `create_vt(header.width as usize, header.height as usize)`
   - Convert markers to effective time: iterate `recording.events` by index, for each `EventType::Marker`, look up `time_map.effective_time(i)` and build a `Marker { time: effective_time, label }`.
   - Initialize state: `current_time = 0.0`, `current_event_index = 0`, `playing = false`, `speed = 1.0`.

6. Implement the internal replay method `fn replay_to(&mut self, target_time: f64)`:
   - Find nearest keyframe: `self.index.keyframe_at(target_time)`.
   - **If found**: get the keyframe, restore Vt via `keyframe.snapshot.restore()`, set `self.current_event_index = keyframe.event_index`.
   - **If not found** (target before first keyframe, or empty index): create a fresh Vt via `create_vt(header.width, header.height)`, set `self.current_event_index = 0`.
   - Replace `self.vt` with the restored/fresh Vt.
   - Feed events forward from `current_event_index`:
     ```rust
     while self.current_event_index < self.recording.events.len() {
         let Some(t) = self.time_map.effective_time(self.current_event_index) else { break };
         if t > target_time { break; }
         match &self.recording.events[self.current_event_index] {
             // Output: vt.feed_str(&data)
             // Resize: vt.feed_str(&format!("\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t"))
             // Input/Marker: skip
         }
         self.current_event_index += 1;
     }
     ```
     Note: the condition is `t > target_time` (i.e., include events AT target_time). After the loop, `current_event_index` points to the first event NOT yet processed.
   - Set `self.current_time = target_time`.

7. Implement `seek(&mut self, time: f64)`:
   - Clamp `time` to `[0.0, self.duration()]`.
   - Call `self.replay_to(clamped_time)`.

8. Implement `seek_relative(&mut self, delta: f64)`:
   - `self.seek(self.current_time + delta)`.

9. Implement accessors:
   - `screen(&self) -> &[avt::Line]` — returns `self.vt.view()`
   - `cursor(&self) -> CursorState` — returns `CursorState::from_vt(&self.vt)`
   - `size(&self) -> (u16, u16)` — `let (c, r) = self.vt.size(); (c as u16, r as u16)`
   - `current_time(&self) -> f64`
   - `duration(&self) -> f64` — returns `self.time_map.duration()`
   - `markers(&self) -> &[Marker]` — returns `&self.markers`

10. Add `pub mod player;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use player::{Player, PlayerError, LoadOptions};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `PlayerError` with `Display`/`Error`/`From` impls for both inner error types
- [ ] `LoadOptions` with `Default` impl
- [ ] `Player::load()` and `load_with()` successfully load all valid test data files
- [ ] Idle limit resolution: CLI override > header `idle_time_limit` > no limit
- [ ] `seek()` clamps to [0, duration] and restores correct terminal state
- [ ] `seek_relative()` works for positive and negative deltas
- [ ] `screen()` returns `&[avt::Line]` from the current Vt view
- [ ] `cursor()` returns correct `CursorState`
- [ ] `size()` returns current Vt dimensions as `(u16, u16)`
- [ ] `duration()`, `current_time()`, `markers()` return correct values
- [ ] Marker times are effective (not raw)
- [ ] Module added to `lib.rs` with re-exports
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
notes:
## Codebase context

**avt API (verified from source):**
- `Vt::view() -> &[avt::Line]` — no `avt::Screen` type exists. Adapt the spec's `screen() -> &avt::Screen` accordingly.
- `Vt::size() -> (usize, usize)` — returns `(cols, rows)`, cast to `u16` for public API.
- `Vt::cursor() -> avt::Cursor` — has `.col`, `.row`, `.visible` fields. `CursorState::from_vt()` already extracts these.
- `Vt::feed_str() -> (Vec<usize>, bool)` — ignore the return value for playback.

**Resize parameter order**: `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }` but xtwinops is `\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t`. Same pattern as in the keyframe indexer.

**Marker conversion**: `Recording.markers` has raw times. Iterate `recording.events` by index to find `EventType::Marker` events and look up `time_map.effective_time(event_index)` for each. The parser ensures markers appear in both `events` and `markers` vecs, but the events vec gives us the index for TimeMap lookup.

**`replay_to()` event inclusion**: Events with `effective_time <= target_time` are included (fed to Vt). After replay, `current_event_index` points to the first event NOT yet processed. This ensures `tick()` (next ticket) doesn't double-process events.

## Verification commands

```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.5.2 — Playback control, tick, and stepping
description:
Implement continuous playback advancement (tick), playback state control (play/pause/speed), frame timing for the event loop, and single-event stepping.

## Tasks

1. **Playback state control:**

   ```rust
   pub fn play(&mut self)          // sets playing = true
   pub fn pause(&mut self)         // sets playing = false
   pub fn toggle(&mut self)        // flips playing
   pub fn is_playing(&self) -> bool
   pub fn set_speed(&mut self, speed: f64)
   pub fn speed(&self) -> f64
   ```

2. **`tick(&mut self, dt: f64) -> bool`** — advance playback by `dt` wall-clock seconds:

   - If not playing, return `false` immediately.
   - Compute `new_time = self.current_time + dt * self.speed`.
   - Clamp `new_time` to `self.duration()`.
   - Track whether any events were processed (`state_changed = false`).
   - Feed events using the same loop pattern as `replay_to`:
     ```rust
     while self.current_event_index < self.recording.events.len() {
         let Some(t) = self.time_map.effective_time(self.current_event_index) else { break };
         if t > new_time { break; }
         // Output: feed to vt, state_changed = true
         // Resize: feed to vt, state_changed = true
         // Input/Marker: skip
         self.current_event_index += 1;
     }
     ```
   - Update `self.current_time = new_time`.
   - **Auto-pause at end**: if `new_time >= self.duration()`, set `self.playing = false` and `state_changed = true` (so the TUI re-renders the final frame with paused state).
   - Return `state_changed`.

3. **`time_to_next_event(&self) -> Option<std::time::Duration>`** — for the TUI event loop:

   - If not playing, return `None`.
   - If `current_event_index >= events.len()`, return `None` (at end).
   - Get the next event's effective time: `time_map.effective_time(current_event_index)`.
   - Compute wall-clock delta: `(next_effective_time - current_time) / speed`.
   - If delta <= 0 (event is overdue), return `Some(Duration::ZERO)`.
   - Convert positive delta to `Duration` and return `Some(duration)`.

4. **`step_forward(&mut self) -> bool`** — advance to the next output event:

   - If `self.playing`, return `false` (stepping only when paused).
   - Scan from `current_event_index` forward, looking for `EventType::Output`. Process each event as you scan:
     - `Resize`: feed to Vt (resizes affect Vt state and must not be skipped).
     - `Input` / `Marker`: skip (no Vt state change).
     - `Output`: feed to Vt, update `current_time` to this event's effective time, set `current_event_index` past it, return `true`.
   - If loop exhausts all events without finding an output event, return `false` (at end).

5. **`step_backward(&mut self) -> bool`** — seek to the previous output event:

   - If `self.playing`, return `false` (stepping only when paused).
   - **Guard against underflow**: if `current_event_index == 0`, return `false` (already at start, no previous event exists). Do NOT compute `current_event_index - 1` without this check — it panics on usize underflow.
   - Scan backward from `current_event_index - 1` toward 0, looking for `EventType::Output`.
   - If found: call `self.seek(effective_time_of_that_event)`. This rebuilds from a keyframe since avt is forward-only.
   - If no previous output event found, return `false`.
   - Return `true`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `play()`/`pause()`/`toggle()`/`is_playing()` work correctly
- [ ] `set_speed()`/`speed()` work correctly
- [ ] `tick()` advances time by `dt × speed`, feeds events to Vt, returns whether state changed
- [ ] `tick()` returns `false` when not playing
- [ ] `tick()` auto-pauses at end of recording, returns `true` for the final frame
- [ ] Subsequent `tick()` calls after reaching end return `false`
- [ ] `time_to_next_event()` returns correct wall-clock `Duration` accounting for speed
- [ ] `time_to_next_event()` returns `None` when paused or at end
- [ ] `step_forward()` advances to next output event, processing intervening resizes
- [ ] `step_forward()` skips input/marker events
- [ ] `step_forward()` returns `false` at end of recording
- [ ] `step_backward()` seeks to previous output event via keyframe replay
- [ ] `step_backward()` returns `false` at start of recording (including when `current_event_index == 0`)
- [ ] Both step methods return `false` when `is_playing()` is true
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
notes:
## Codebase context

**Depends on 1.5.1**: The `Player` struct, `replay_to()`, `seek()`, and all accessors already exist. This ticket adds playback advancement methods.

**`tick()` performance**: Called on every frame. The hot path processes only events in the `[current_time, new_time]` window — typically 1–3 events. `vt.feed_str()` handles these in microseconds.

**`step_backward()` cost**: Internally calls `seek()`, which restores from a keyframe and replays forward. Bounded by `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` (5s of events) — still fast but more expensive than `step_forward()`.

**`time_to_next_event()` is the TUI's sleep duration**: The event loop calls `crossterm::event::poll(timeout)` with this value. Returning `None` means the loop uses a default idle poll interval (100ms). Returning `Duration::ZERO` means "there's an event ready right now."

**Event processing in `step_forward`**: Unlike `tick()` which skips non-output events silently, `step_forward` must process intervening resize events so the Vt dimensions stay correct. It steps through events one by one until it finds the next output event.

## Verification commands

```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.5.3 — Player tests
description:
Comprehensive test suite for all Player behavior: loading, seeking, playback, stepping, and metadata.

## Tasks

1. **Load tests:**
   - Smoke test: load all valid test data files in a loop (`minimal_v2.cast`, `minimal_v3.cast`, `empty.cast`, `long_idle.cast`, `with_markers.cast`, `with_resize.cast`, `alternate_buffer.cast`, `real_session.cast`) — verify no error and non-panic. This is one test function, not eight.
   - Idle limit resolution: load `long_idle.cast` with explicit `idle_limit` override via `LoadOptions` — verify duration differs from loading without override. Load without override — verify header's `idle_time_limit` is applied (duration should be shorter than raw).
   - Error cases: empty bytes and binary garbage — verify appropriate `PlayerError` variant.

2. **Seek tests** (including screen content verification):
   - `seek(0.0)` — verify cursor at origin, screen is blank (initial state).
   - `seek(known_event_time)` — load `minimal_v2.cast`, seek to the effective time of the last event, verify `screen()[0].text()` contains expected output.
   - `seek(between_events)` — verify state reflects events up to but not past target time.
   - `seek(duration + 100.0)` — clamps to duration.
   - `seek(-5.0)` — clamps to 0.
   - `seek_relative(+delta)` and `seek_relative(-delta)` — verify `current_time()` updates correctly.
   - Seek forward then backward — verify state is consistent (no accumulation artifacts from forward-only Vt).

3. **Tick tests:**
   - Start playing, `tick(small_dt)` — verify events are fed, `current_time()` advances, returns `true` if events were processed.
   - `tick(dt)` at 2× speed — verify `current_time()` advances by `2 * dt`.
   - `tick()` when paused — returns `false`, `current_time()` unchanged.
   - `tick()` that reaches end — auto-pauses (`is_playing() == false`), returns `true`. Subsequent `tick()` returns `false`.

4. **`time_to_next_event` tests:**
   - At start, playing at 1× — returns time to first event's effective time.
   - At 2× speed — returns half the time.
   - When paused or at end — returns `None`.

5. **Stepping tests:**
   - `step_forward()` from start while paused — advances to first output event, returns `true`.
   - `step_forward()` skips input/marker events (use `with_markers.cast`).
   - `step_forward()` at end — returns `false`.
   - `step_backward()` from mid-recording — goes to previous output event, verify `current_time()` reflects that event.
   - `step_backward()` at start — returns `false`.
   - Both step methods while playing — return `false`.

6. **Marker and insta snapshot tests:**
   - Load `with_markers.cast` — verify `markers()` returns 2 markers with correct labels and effective times (not raw).
   - Load `minimal_v2.cast`, seek to end, snapshot the screen text lines. Locks down the full pipeline: parse → time map → index → seek → render.

title: Epic 2.2 — TerminalView Widget
description:
Implement the ratatui TerminalView widget that renders avt's terminal cell grid to the host terminal. This is a pure rendering component with no side effects — it maps avt cells to ratatui cells with correct colors, attributes, cursor rendering, and viewport scrolling.

## Context

The core engine (speedrun-core) provides `player.screen() -> &[avt::Line]` which gives access to the terminal cell grid. Each `avt::Line` contains `Cell(char, Pen)` tuples. The TerminalView widget must map these to ratatui buffer cells.

### Key design decisions

- **Cell-by-cell mapping** using `line.cells()` iterator which yields `(char, Pen)` pairs.
- **Color mapping** is direct: `avt::Color::Indexed(u8)` → `ratatui::Color::Indexed(u8)`, `avt::Color::RGB(RGB8)` → `ratatui::Color::Rgb(r, g, b)`, `None` → `Color::Reset`.
- **Attribute mapping**: `Pen` has `is_bold()`, `is_faint()`, `is_italic()`, `is_underline()`, `is_strikethrough()`, `is_blink()`, `is_inverse()` which map to ratatui `Modifier` flags.
- **Wide characters**: `char_width()` via `unicode-width` — wide chars occupy 2 cells, so the second cell must be skipped.
- **Cursor rendering**: if visible, apply `REVERSED` modifier at cursor position.
- **Viewport scrolling**: when host terminal is smaller than recording, the viewport follows the cursor.

### avt API surface (verified)

- `avt::Line::cells() -> impl Iterator<Item = (char, Pen)>` — cell-by-cell with character and style
- `avt::Cell(char, Pen)` — tuple struct, Cell is `pub(crate)` but accessed through `Line::cells()`
- `avt::Pen` — style information:
  - `foreground() -> Option<Color>`, `background() -> Option<Color>`
  - `is_bold()`, `is_faint()`, `is_italic()`, `is_underline()`, `is_strikethrough()`, `is_blink()`, `is_inverse()`
- `avt::Color` — `Indexed(u8) | RGB(rgb::RGB8)` where `RGB8 { r: u8, g: u8, b: u8 }`
- `avt::Vt::view() -> &[Line]` — the visible line grid
- `avt::Vt::cursor() -> Cursor` — `{ col: usize, row: usize, visible: bool }`

### ratatui API surface (verified)

- `Widget` trait: `fn render(self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer)`
- `buf.get_mut(x, y) -> &mut Cell` — global coordinates (offset by area.x/area.y)
- `Cell::set_char(ch)`, `set_fg(Color)`, `set_bg(Color)`, `cell.modifier = Modifier::FLAGS`
- `Color::Indexed(u8)`, `Color::Rgb(u8, u8, u8)`, `Color::Reset`
- `Modifier::BOLD | DIM | ITALIC | UNDERLINED | SLOW_BLINK | REVERSED | HIDDEN | CROSSED_OUT`

## Test visibility for private helpers

The test code in this ticket calls `map_color()` and `map_modifiers()` directly. These are private functions in `ui.rs`. To make them testable, use one of these approaches:

**Recommended**: Put tests inside `ui.rs` as a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests {}` block. This gives tests access to private functions. The tests in this ticket should go inside `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs`, not in a separate test file.

```rust
// At the bottom of ui.rs:
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use ratatui::buffer::Buffer;
    // ... all tests here have access to map_color, map_modifiers, etc.
}
```

**Alternative**: Make `map_color` and `map_modifiers` `pub(crate)` instead of private. This is acceptable since they're implementation details of the TUI crate, not part of a public library API.

## `rgb` crate import for tests

The color mapping tests use `rgb::RGB8 { r: 255, g: 128, b: 0 }`. The `rgb` crate must be available. It should already be in `Cargo.toml` (added in 2.2.1 or 2.1.1). Verify with:
```rust
use rgb::RGB8;
```

## Test data paths

Tests reference `../../testdata/minimal_v2.cast`. This path is relative to `crates/speedrun/src/` (two levels up from the source file to the workspace root, then into `testdata/`). However, `cargo test` runs from the crate root (`crates/speedrun/`), so the correct relative path is `../../testdata/minimal_v2.cast`. This matches how `speedrun-core` tests use the same pattern.

## `create_vt` is public

`speedrun_core::create_vt(cols, rows)` is re-exported from `lib.rs` and available for test usage. This is how the attribute mapping tests construct a Vt to feed SGR sequences to.

## avt dependency needed for tests

Tests import `avt::Color` for constructing test values (e.g., `avt::Color::Indexed(1)`). The `avt` dependency must be in `Cargo.toml` (should have been added in 2.2.1).
